mnemonics.title: Digital Design Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: ABS-RLC: Abstraction Levels - System → Behavioral → Register-Transfer → Logic → Circuit → Physical

mnemonics.title: Design Metrics
mnemonics.content: CAP-P: Cost, Area (or Power), Performance - pick two due to trade-offs

mnemonics.title: Architecture vs Organization
mnemonics.content: Arch-O: Architecture is the 'A' for 'Attribute' (what), Organization is the 'O' for 'Operation' (how)

acronyms.usage: Languages like Verilog and VHDL used to model digital systems at various abstraction levels, enabling simulation and synthesis.
Abstraction level where operations describe data flow between registers, typically using clock cycles for synchronization.
The three primary optimization metrics in modern digital design; improving one often requires trade-offs in others.
Device that converts continuous analog signals to discrete digital values through sampling and quantization.
One-time costs in digital design including design, verification, and mask generation, amortized over production volume.
Reconfigurable digital hardware that can implement various logic functions after manufacturing, used for prototyping and specialized applications.

visualTips.visualization: Pyramid with System at top (wide, abstract), narrowing down through Behavioral, RTL, Logic, Circuit to Physical at bottom (detailed). Each level 'refines' the one above.
Waveform showing smooth continuous curve for analog (like a sine wave), versus stepped square wave for digital - the 'stairs' representing discrete binary samples.

Two overlapping circles - 'Architecture' (left) labeled 'What: Instruction Set, Registers, Addressing' and 'Organization' (right) labeled 'How: ALU implementation, Cache structure, Bus design'. Large overlap shows they're related but distinct.
Voltage transfer curve showing two distinct 'hills' for logic 0 and logic 1 regions with a wide gap (undefined region) between them - this gap represents the noise margin.

analogies.analogy: Like architectural blueprints: System level = site plan, Behavioral = floor plan showing room functions, RTL = plumbing/electrical layout, Logic = switch and outlet locations, Circuit = actual wire connections, Physical = final built structure.
Like a restaurant: Architecture is the menu (what dishes you can order), Organization is the kitchen layout and staff organization (how the food is actually prepared and served).

Like a light switch - only two positions (on/off), making it reliable and unambiguous. Even though a dimmer switch (analog) offers infinite settings, the simple on/off is easier to control and understand.

Like a musical score for an orchestra - it describes the music (behavior) in a standard notation that musicians (synthesis tools) can interpret and convert into actual performances (hardware implementations).
